texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrogen (H2) separation and purification is challenging because of the high purity and recovery requirements in particular applications, as well as the critical properties of H2 and its associated components. Unlike pressure swing adsorption, cryogenic- and membrane-based technologies are currently employed for H2  separation. Membraneassisted (case-I) and cryogenic-assisted (case-II) separation and purification of H2  were evaluated in this study in terms of the energy, exergy, and economic aspects of the processes. In case-I and case-II, H2 was first produced from synthesis gas via the water-gas shift reaction and was then separated from other components using membrane and cryogenic systems, respectively. Additionally, an organic Rankine cycle was integrated with the water-gas shift reactors to recover the waste heat. A well-known commercial process simulation software, Aspen Hysys ® v11, was employed to simulate both processes. Energy analysis revealed that case-I has a lower energy consumption (0.50 kWh/ kg) than case-II (2.01 kWh/kg). However, low H2 purity and recovery rates are the main limitations of case-I. In terms of exergy, the H2 separation section in case-I exhibited a higher efficiency (28.4%) than case-II (14.7%). Furthermore, the economic evaluation showed that case-I was more expensive ($17.7 M) than case-II ($10.2 M) because of the high cost of the compressors required. In conclusion, this study could assist industry practitioners and academic researchers in selecting optimal H2 separation and purification technologies for improving the overall H2 economy.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The impact of climate change and carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions has led the scientific community and governments worldwide to adopt extreme corrective measures. Many countries are currently focused on limiting the global temperature rise to 1.5 ◦ C by increasing the usage of renewable carbon-free energy sources and hydrogen (H2) (IEA, 2021). H2 is the lightest, most abundant, and cleanest fuel worldwide. The industrial production of H2 is based on the steam reforming of natural gas (approximately  48%),  oil/naphtha  (approximately  30%),  and  coal gasification (approximately 18%) (Barei β et al., 2019). The synthesis gas or syngas (SG) produced by these processes is a promising candidate for use in chemical-, fuel-, and power-production technologies. SG consists of carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen (N2), CO2, and H2, with different compositions depending on the technology and raw material used (He et al., 2022). Downstream H2 separation and purification is a crucial step for obtaining high-purity H2 gas.
texts.text: The impact of climate change and carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions has led the scientific community and governments worldwide to adopt extreme corrective measures. Many countries are currently focused on limiting the global temperature rise to 1.5 ◦ C by increasing the usage of renewable carbon-free energy sources and hydrogen (H2) (IEA, 2021). H2 is the lightest, most abundant, and cleanest fuel worldwide. The industrial production of H2 is based on the steam reforming of natural gas (approximately  48%),  oil/naphtha  (approximately  30%),  and  coal gasification (approximately 18%) (Barei β et al., 2019). The synthesis gas or syngas (SG) produced by these processes is a promising candidate for use in chemical-, fuel-, and power-production technologies. SG consists of carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen (N2), CO2, and H2, with different compositions depending on the technology and raw material used (He et al., 2022). Downstream H2 separation and purification is a crucial step for obtaining high-purity H2 gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the perspective of a H2-based economy, the production of H2 with ultra-high purity ( > 99.95%) is an important requirement (see the ISO  14687 -2:2012  standard)  (Chen  and  Lin,  2013).  Numerous  techniques  have  been  explored  for  H2 separation,  including  absorption, adsorption,  cryogenic  separation,  and  membrane  separation  (Kazmi et al., 2022). Currently, adsorption-based technologies are widely utilized for the separation and purification of H2  at industrial scale, with pressure  swing  adsorption  (PSA)  being  the  most  common  choice. Although high purity is achieved via PSA, the recovery rate of approximately 80%, owing to the high losses during the desorption step, remains  a  major  limitation  (Bernardo  et  al.,  2020a).  Therefore,  more innovative strategies are being explored. Liu et al. (2020) introduced the concept of steam rinsing and purging beyond the dew point to enhance the  purity  and  recovery  rates  of  H2  at  elevated  temperatures  in  PSA units. These process modifications resulted in a purity of 99.98% and a recovery rate of 99.33%. The PSA-membrane hybrid configuration reported by Li et al. (2016) achieved 99.98% purity and 91.71% recovery. Further  efforts  are  ongoing  to  modify  and  develop  effective  and economical H2 purification technologies, particularly on a large scale.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among other promising alternative technologies is cryogenic separation, which is a relatively new and can be applied with and without distillation.  The  common  process  step  in  both  variations  is  partial condensation.  The  required  cooling  can  be  provided via the  JouleThomson  effect,  external  refrigeration/liquefaction  cycles,  or  turboexpansion of the produced H2. Under cryogenic conditions, all gases (such as CH4, CO, and N2) condense, leaving pure H2 with a comparatively high recovery rate exceeding 98% (Vermaak et al., 2021). This method  is  generally  used  to  separate  H2 from  light  hydrocarbon mixtures (Du et al., 2021). However, the removal of water and CO2 from the gaseous mixture is essential to avoid solidification, choking/blocking, and equipment damage. Yurata et al. (2019) proposed the cryogenic de-sublimation of CO2 from a gaseous mixture, integrating with liquid H2 process.  Naquash  et  al.  (2022)  also  applied  a  similar  concept. Ghorbani et al. (2020) used cryogenic coolers and a membrane separation unit to produce H2 with 99.99% purity. Their study was focused on the process development of a multi-production facility. Cryogenic separation without distillation is advantageous because of its high recovery rate and low operating cost; however, the purity of the produced H2 is compromised. The addition of a distillation column improves the purity  ( > 99.5%)  but  increases  the  operation  costs  and  energy  consumption (Dehghani et al., 2012). Cryogenic distillation for separating and removing H2 during the production of liquid N2 was patented by Ha et al. (1997).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently,  membrane  separation  has  been  extensively  applied  in industrial-scale technologies. It is based on differences in partial pressures across the membrane and has the potential to overcome the previously discussed challenges associated with PSA. Membrane processes have  multiple  advantages,  such  as  easy  operation,  high  energy  efficiency,  and  easy  scale-up  (Tawalbeh  et  al.,  2021).  Numerous  membranes,  including  polymeric,  mixed-matrix,  carbon  molecular  sieve, ionic-liquid-based,  palladium-based,  inorganic,  and  electrochemical membranes, have been developed for H2  separation and purification (Bernardo  et  al.,  2020a).  The  analysis  of  PSA  and  membrane-based stand-alone  and  hybrid  multi-configuration  systems  for  purifying  H2 generated from ammonia at fueling stations showed a recovery rate and purity  of > 95% and 99.5%,  respectively.  The  results  for  the  hybrid schemes were encouraging and superior to those for the stand-alone configurations.  Although  the  as-obtained  characteristic  parameters represented  a  significant  improvement  with  respect  to  the  industrial values, they were far less than the targeted values (Lin et al., 2020).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Extensive  literature  on  membranes  is  available  regarding  the removal of CO2, H2S, CH4, N2, and other light hydrocarbons (Liu et al., 2022). However, the available information on H2 separation and purification from SG is relatively limited, especially from a process systems engineering perspective. Table 1 compares the accessible literature with some useful information, such as the technology used, feed treated, and the benchmark characteristics of H2 purity and recovery. Experimental studies  were  not  considered  in  this  literature  review.  Some  studies focused on energy savings instead of H2  purity or recovery. Similarly, detailed  analytical  comparisons  of  the  available  technologies  remain scarce (Wickham et al., 2022).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: technical  and  economic  feasibility.  Generally,  a  chemical  process  is evaluated by considering detailed design information, such as total energy  consumption,  exergy  efficiency,  capital  investment,  operating costs, and overall process complexity. This set of information is vital for the selection of an optimum separation and purification process for H2. Therefore, this study represents an attempt in this direction. More specifically, this study is the first of its kind that provides extensive technical details  on  cryogenic-  and  membrane-based  separation  and purification processes as well as the overall picture when these processes are integrated with those upstream, such as the water-gas shift (WGS) reaction.  Conventional  approaches  have  also  been  modified  where possible to save energy. To the best of the authors ' knowledge, such a comparative  assessment  of  the  SG  downstream  processes  integrated with membrane-based and cryogenic-based H2  separation and purification approaches is not available in the open literature, especially from a technical, thermodynamic, and economic perspective.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separations  based  on α values  and  phase  behavior  are  relatively simple and widely applied in the gas industry. In the case of H2 recovery from mixtures with N2, CO, and CO2, the significant difference in the boiling points of H2  and N2  ( i.e. , 57.1 ◦ C) renders a feasible VLE-based separation. However, low temperatures may induce the solidification of CO2  in the mixture because its freezing point ( 78.5 ◦ C) is significantly higher than the boiling point of H2  ( 252.9 ◦ C) at atmospheric pressure. Increasing the pressure to 5.5 bar shifts the system away from the solidification boundary of CO2, but towards the critical pressure of H2. Therefore, CO2  must first be removed from the mixture. After CO2 removal, the remaining mixture (H2, N2, and CO) can be separated via cryogenic distillation. The behavior of this CO2-free mixture can also be understood using the ternary diagram shown in Fig. 1.

However, low temperatures may induce the solidification of CO2  in the mixture because its freezing point ( 78.5 ◦ C) is significantly higher than the boiling point of H2  ( 252.9 ◦ C) at atmospheric pressure. Increasing the pressure to 5.5 bar shifts the system away from the solidification boundary of CO2, but towards the critical pressure of H2. Therefore, CO2  must first be removed from the mixture. After CO2 removal, the remaining mixture (H2, N2, and CO) can be separated via cryogenic distillation. The behavior of this CO2-free mixture can also be understood using the ternary diagram shown in Fig. 1.

texts.orig: In contrast, membrane-based separation shows great promise for the separation of H2 and CO2 (Y ´ a ˜ nez et al., 2020). This technology has the advantage  of  easy  operation,  compact  configuration,  as  well  as  low operation  cost  and  environmental  impact.  However,  achieving  high purity  with  high  recovery  is  challenging  with  membrane  separation. Two types of selective membranes for the separation of H2  and CO2 determine  the  target  purity  and  recovery.  Generally,  CO2-selective membranes are favorable to achieving high H2 purity with a moderate H2 recovery  rate,  while  H2-selective  membranes  are  suitable  for attaining high H2 recovery with moderate H2 purity (Y ´ a ˜ nez et al., 2020). In this study, the proportion of H2  is the highest in the mixture; therefore, a CO2-selective membrane  was  selected for separation. A polymer-type  membrane,  i.e.,  polyethylene  glycol  dimethyl  ether (PEGDME), with permeabilities of CO2  and H2  of 814 and 74 barrers, respectively, and a CO2/H2  mixed gas selectivity of 11.1, was adopted (Barillas et  al.,  2011).  In  industrial  membrane  applications,  the spiral-wound module is one of the most commonly used strategies for separating CO2 (Wang et al., 2018). It is designed following a cross-flow pattern [27] that approximates the actual spiral-wound separator. The gas  permeation  transport  mechanism  in  the  dense  membrane  is described  using  the  solution-diffusion  model  (Baker  et  al.,  2010),  as shown in Equation (3), where Ji (m 3 (STP)/m 2 h) is the flux of component i ; Di (cm 2 /s) is the diffusion coefficient; K G i (cm 3 (STP)/cm 3  cm ⋅ Hg) is the sorption coefficient; l is the membrane thickness; pio and pil are the partial pressures of component i on each side of the membrane ( i.e. , on the surfaces o and l ), and P G i is the gas permeability coefficient.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ratio of the diffusion coefficients DH 2 / DCO 2 is determined by the relative  sizes  of  the  molecules.  Therefore,  the  permeation  of  smaller molecules  is  always  faster  than  that  of  larger  ones.  The  ratio  of  the sorption  coefficients KN 2 / KCH 4 determines  the  sorption  of  the  more condensable component. The mobility and sorption selectivity depend on the polymer type (Lokhandwala et al., 2010); therefore, their effects on the separation of H2 and CO2 are not similar. Because H2 is a smaller molecule and has a lower boiling point than CO2 (Orme et al., 2006), its permeance is faster than that of CO2 in a glassy polymer membrane but slower in a rubbery polymer membrane. In the rubbery polymer membrane,  the  permeability  of  N2 is  approximately  equal  to  that  of  H2, whereas H2O exhibits the fastest permeance among all components in the mixture. In this study, owing to the low concentrations of the other components, their permeabilities were mainly assumed. For example, the  permeability  of  N2  was  assumed  to  be  similar  to  that  of  H2,  the permeability of H2O was considered faster than that of CO2, and CO was assumed to be rejected by the membrane (as the retentate).

The SG stream  (2)  then  enters  a  high-temperature  shift  converter  (HTSC)  to promote  the  high-temperature  WGS  reaction.  The  WGS  reaction (Equation (5)) is reversible and exothermic (Gradisher et al., 2015).

texts.label: formula

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Industrially, this reaction is conducted in two stages: The first stage takes place in the temperature range of 350 -450 ◦ C, and the second stage occurs in the temperature range of 150 -250 ◦ C (Gao et al., 2019). The high-temperature SG stream (2) is reacted with high-pressure steam at 42 bar and 264 ◦ C in the HTSC. The top stream (3), leaving the HTSC at 396.7 ◦ C, is cooled to 220 ◦ C by passing through a multi-stream heat exchanger (MSHX-1). Thereafter, stream (4) enters a low-temperature shift converter (LTSC) to further convert CO to H2. The temperatures in the HTSC and LTSC were carefully adjusted to promote high conversions. Details on the WGS reaction and the relationship of conversion with temperature can be found in (Gao et al., 2019). Stream (5) at the outlet of the LTSC, with 50% H2 at a temperature of 217 ◦ C, is cooled by passing through MSHX-2. The excess water is removed using a phase separator (V-1); thereafter, the product SG stream (7) is directed to the membrane unit for H2  separation and purification. Because the WGS reaction  is  exothermic,  the  excess  heat  removed  using  MSHX-1  and MSHX-2 is utilized to generate power using an organic Rankine cycle (ORC) with n-pentane as a working fluid. The working fluid is pumped at 32 bar, which is less than its critical pressure (Pcritical = 33 bar). Subsequently, it enters MSHX-1 and MSHX-2 to increase its temperature to 273 ◦ C before entering the turbine (T-1). The high-temperature working fluid is expanded isentropically to generate power. From T-1, 32.8 MW of power is produced. After expansion to 1 bar, stream (28) is subsequently cooled and condensed in E 1 and E 3 to complete the ORC.

texts.orig: In case-II, the WGS reaction and ORC system sections (indicated by the green dashed box in Figs. 2 and 4) are the same as those in case-I. The only differences are in the working fluid flow rate and LTSC inlet temperature. In case-II, the working fluid flow rate was 334 kg/s, whereas the LTSC inlet temperature was 205 ◦ C. These values were adjusted to obtain  the  desired  minimum  internal  temperature  approach  (MITA) value (that is greater than 3.0 ◦ C). The remaining section in the diagram, which  involves  removing  additional  water  and  separating  H2,  is explained here. Stream (7) is compressed to 80 bar and cooled to 20 ◦ C in  E 4.  The  excess  water  is  removed  before  cryogenic  processing  to avoid freezing at low temperatures. For this purpose, a triethylene glycol (TEG) process is adopted to remove the water. TEG absorption is a wellknown process for water removal, in which high-pressure SG is absorbed by TEG in an absorber. The bottom TEG-rich stream (12) is sent to the TEG regenerator to recover the TEG, which is then recycled back to the absorber. The top stream of the absorber unit (11) enters V-4 at 24.6 ◦ C and  80  bar.  A  detailed  description  of  the  TEG  unit  can  be  found  in (Chukwuma Godwin Jacob, 2014). The dry, H2-rich SG stream (22) is then directed to the cryogenic unit. The stream temperature and pressure are first reduced to 58 ◦ C and 4.5 bar, respectively, to convert CO2 from a vapor to a solid, followed by the removal of the solid CO2. This way, the freezing of CO2 in the distillation column is avoided. The solid CO2  is  removed  using  a  cold  box,  while  CO2-free  SG  is  sent  to  the distillation column. The conditions for CO2 solidification were selected from (Naquash et al., 2021). The SG stream (25) comprising CO, N2, and H2 is sent to the cryogenic distillation column to separate H2 with a high purity and recovery. H2 is extracted from the top, while CO and N2 are separated from the bottom. The energy required to decrease the temperature of the SG stream for cryogenic processing is provided by the refrigeration cycle. In this cycle, a mixture of C1  and C2  is first compressed to 12 bar in a series of compressors, then, the refrigerant stream (30) is cooled in E 8 and MSHX-3. The cooled stream (32) is expanded isentropically in T-3 to 2.6 bar and directed back to MSHX-3 to provide energy to the refrigerant and SG streams. After the heat exchange, the refrigerant stream (27) is recycled to complete the refrigeration cycle. The detailed stream conditions of case-II are provided in the Supplementary material (Table S2).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Exergy analysis
texts.text: 3.4. Exergy analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Exergy  analysis  is  an  important  tool  based  on  the  second  law  of thermodynamics. An energy analysis only calculates the energy consumption  and  inefficiencies  of  an  equipment;  however,  it  does  not determine the source or reason for the inefficiencies. In contrast, an exergy analysis determines and locates the sources of the process inefficiencies  and  thermodynamic  losses.  Depending  on  the  mode  of operation,  exergy is  mainly categorized into two types: physical and chemical. Physical exergy is based on the temperature and pressure of the system, whereas chemical exergy is related to the composition of mixtures. Therefore, physical exergy changes when the temperature or pressure of a stream changes, whereas chemical exergy is unaffected by temperature and pressure because it only changes when the composition of the stream changes. In this study, a reaction is involved; therefore, the chemical exergies of the streams were also calculated. texts.orig: The condenser and reboiler duties in the TEG unit play an important role in the overall SEC and are related to the TEG flow rate, as shown in Fig. 7. By increasing the TEG mass flow rate, the condenser duty decreases,  while  the  reboiler  duty  increases.  This  is  because  a  larger reboiler duty is required to regenerate TEG from the process stream, while the recovery of H2O in the top stream decreases, consequently decreasing the condenser duty and increasing the H2O content in the dry product gas (stream 11). For this reason, the optimal value of the mass flow rate of TEG (1.56 kg/s) was chosen to produce a dry product gas with low reboiler and condenser duties.

texts.orig: Membrane area and operating pressure also determine the overall process efficiency. In case-I, the pressure and area of Mem-2 are key variables in the analysis of H2 purity and energy consumption. With an increasing membrane area, the H2  purity and energy consumption are also increased. After reaching an area of 5000 m 2 , the slope of the purity curve  decreases.  This  implies  that  to  achieve  a  purity  higher  than 0.9886 at or above 5000 m 2 ,  the  energy  consumption must increase rapidly. In other words, for an area larger than 5000 m 2 , the purity per unit of energy is lower than that of a smaller membrane area. Therefore, the selected optimum area value is 5000 m 2 . The operating pressure of the membrane is selected in a similar manner, as shown in Fig. 8(b). The optimum pressure is 10.13 bar, at which the H2 purity is 0.9886.

Apart from the columns, caseII exhibits lower exergy destruction than case-I except for MSHXs. The MSHXs show a high exergy destruction in case-II than in case-I, owing to the  additional  MSHX  ( i.e. ,  MSHX-3).  Furthermore,  the  section  with membranes introduces 70.8 MW (approximately 48%) in overall exergy destruction in case-I. This is because of the compressors in the membrane section. Furthermore, the pumps and reactors show similar exergy destruction values in both cases because of the similar process conditions. The high exergy destruction in columns and membranes in both cases can be reduced by further optimizing the design variables for the distillation  and  membrane  sections.  This  would  enhance  the  overall exergy efficiency of the process.

texts.orig: Additionally, Table 11 shows a comparison of the equipment cost for both cases. The total equipment cost for case-II is 41% lower than that for case-I. As mentioned earlier, the compressors and membranes are the main contributors to the total equipment cost of case-I, while the compressors and MSHXs are the main contributors in case-II. The high energy consumption of the compressors, associated with the refrigeration cycle  in  case-II,  is  the  main  reason  for  the  high  cost;  therefore, decreasing this energy consumption may reduce the equipment costs. Furthermore, the expander in the refrigeration cycle produces 8289 kW of  power,  thereby  reducing  the  overall  net  energy  consumption  and consequently decreasing the OC in case-II.

texts.text: A section-based comparison of the equipment costs for case-I and case-II is presented in Fig. 11.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  ORC  section  has  similar  total  equipment  costs  in  both  cases because of the similar operating conditions. The 0.2 m$ cost difference between the ORC sections in case-I and case-II is due to the flow rate of the WF, which is 28% higher in case-II than in case-I. The WGS section in case-I shows a higher equipment cost than in case-II because of the high costs of MSHX-1 and MSHX-2 in case-I. In other words, the MSHXs in case-I require a larger area than those in case-II. The area of the MSHXs depends on the MITA value. The higher the MITA values, the lower are the required area and cost ( i.e. , Q = UA Δ T ). Moreover, the TEG section has a cost of 2.7 m$, which largely comprises the cost of the compressor (K-1) of 2.4 m$, owing to its high energy consumption. Moreover, the H2 separation  section  in  case-I  ( i.e. ,  membrane  section)  shows  a  significantly higher cost than that in case-II ( i.e. , cryogenic section), owing to the high cost of the compressors in the membrane section, which account for approximately 75% of the cost. In contrast, the membranes account for the remaining approximately 25% of the cost. In case-II, the major share of the equipment cost is due to the refrigeration cycle. This high  cost  of  refrigeration  cycle  is  also  due  to  the  high  energy  consumption of the compressors. However, other parameters, such as H2 purity  and  recovery  rates,  should  be  considered  when  attempting  to reduce the energy consumption of the compressors.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To conclude the above-stated results and discussion, it is imperative to  analyze  both  cases  in  terms  of  energy,  exergy,  and  economics simultaneously. The results obtained show that case-I is advantageous with respect to energy requirements, but from an economics perspective, case-I may not be beneficial because of the high TAC and low product recovery. High product recovery improves product economics, as can be seen in case-II, but the major challenge with case-II is the high energy consumption,  which  consequently  leads  to  high  exergy  destruction. Therefore, there exists a trade-off between these two cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 12 shows that the proposed case-II is efficient in terms of H2 purity and recovery; however, the major limitation is its high SEC. The cryogenic process (Aasadnia et al., 2021) depicts a low H2  purity and recovery; however, the process is relatively energy-efficient because of the self-recuperative process configuration. In (Aasadnia et al., 2021), the authors used the cold energy of the product to provide the required cold duty. Therefore, in this context, the proposed case-II can be further improved by recovering the 'cold ' waste energy of the product.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions and future work
texts.text: 5. Conclusions and future work

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrogen separation and purification technologies have been widely discussed  in  recent  years;  therefore,  a  comparative  evaluation  of  H2 separation  and  purification  processes  was  conducted  in  this  study. Hydrogen, produced from SG through the WGS reaction, is separated and purified through membrane (case-I) and cryogenic distillation (caseII) processes. The process adopted in case-I is more energy- and exergyefficient than that in case-II. Case-I exhibited an SEC of 0.50 kWh/kg, approximately 76% lower than that of case-II. Case-II showed a high SEC because of the elevated heat duties required in the cryogenic section. Moreover, case-I exhibited approximately 58% less exergy destruction than case-II, which had the largest exergy destruction in the cryogenic section  and  an  exergy  efficiency  of  14.7%.  The  economic  analysis revealed that case-I was more cost-intensive than case-II because of the compressors in the membrane section. However, a trade-off exists between these two processes. In other words, although case-II produces high-purity H2 at high recovery rates, its high SEC is a major challenge. This high SEC can be reduced by recovering the cold waste energy of the product or integrating with the cold energy source. In future studies, a detailed advanced exergoeconomic analysis can be conducted for the proposed processes. An advanced exergy analysis can provide a detailed evaluation  of  the  process  inefficiencies.  Furthermore,  the  proposed processes can be integrated into the liquid H2 production process as the cryogenic  process  provides  an  added  benefit  of  cooling.  Finally,  a detailed  environmental  analysis  can  also  be  considered  in  terms  of greenhouse gas emissions to evaluate the environmental impact of this study.
